message.content:
Implementation files: src/claim-office.ts, src/cli.ts (all non-spec *.ts in src/)
Test files: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Passing tests: 36 (all green; 0 todo)

Run the final metric-driven refactoring pass over the whole src/.
Iterate ONE change at a time with pre/post measurement (ESLint smells, SonarJS cognitive complexity, APP mass, McCabe cyclomatic complexity). Keep all 36 tests green after every change (run `pnpm test`). Stop when no metric improves further or no improvement is possible.

Context: This is the MHPCO Claim Office kata. claim-office.ts implements quote premium calculation (base premiums, component blocks, cursed/high-enchantment item surcharges, loyalty/first-insurance/follow-up policy modifiers, processing fee, round up) and claim processing (deductible per damage, enchantment>=8 50% reimbursement, cap = 2x insurance sum tracked cumulatively across successive claims, validation throwing on unknown item type / damage count exceeding insured / negative amount, payout rounded down). cli.ts reads JSON from stdin and writes results to stdout, exiting non-zero with stderr error on throw.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
ESLint config is strict: cognitive complexity ≤10, max-depth 3, max-lines-per-function 30, max-params 4, no-magic-numbers, no-duplicate-string threshold 3. The whole src/ passes with zero smells. This is a strong PRE baseline.

Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe for every function to find the worst offender. Let me analyze each function systematically.

## Iteration 1 — Whole-src PRE Measurement

**ESLint smells (whole src/): 0** (zero across both files)

**Cognitive complexity:** All functions below the threshold of 10 (no SonarJS violations reported).

Now computing **APP mass** and **McCabe** per function. McCabe = 1 + decision points. Let me go function by function.

**claim-office.ts:**

| Function | McCabe | Notes |
|----------|--------|-------|
| `isComponent` | 1 | no branches |
| `typeBasePremium` | 1+1(`&&`)+1(`?:`) = 3 | ternary + `&&` |
| `surchargeForItem` | 1+1(`?:` curse)+1(`??`)+1(`>=?:`) ≈ 4 | `item.cursed ?`, `?? 0`, `>= ? :` |
| `itemSurcharges` | 1 | reduce, no branch in arrow |
| `countBy` | 1+1(for)+1(`??`) = 3 | loop + nullish |
| `countByType` | 1 | |
| `policyBasePremium` | 1 | reduce |
| `isLoyal` | 1 | |
| `policyModifierRate` | 1+1(`?:`)+1(`?:`) = 3 | two ternaries |
| `isKnownType` | 1 | |
| `validateItems` | 1+1(for)+1(if) = 3 | |
| `quotePremium` | 1 | no branches |
| `insuranceSum` | 1 | |
| `qualifiesForHalfReimbursement` | 1+1(`??`) = 2 | |
| `reimbursableAmount` | 1+1(`?:`) = 2 | |
| `damagePayout` | 1 | find, no branch |
| `assertNonNegativeAmounts` | 1+1(for)+1(if) = 3 | |
| `assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts` | 1+1(for)+1(if)+1(`??`) = 4 | |
| `validateDamages` | 1 | |
| `claimResult` | 1 | |
| `processClaim` | 1+1(`??`) = 2 | |
| `runScenario` | 1+1(forEach)+1(if)+1(`>0?`) ≈ 4 | |

**cli.ts:**

| Function | McCabe | Notes |
|----------|--------|-------|
| `readStdin` | 1+1(for-await) = 2 | |
| `main` | 1+1(catch)+1(`instanceof?:`) = 3 | |

Let me compute APP mass for the highest-McCabe candidates. The top McCabe functions are tied at 4: `surchargeForItem`, `assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts`, `runScenario`. Let me compute their APP mass to break ties.

Let me look closely at these three to assess refactoring potential.

**`runScenario`** (McCabe 4) — APP mass estimate:
- bindings: `policiesByStep`, `quoteCount`, `results`, `step`, `index`, `isFollowup` ≈ 6
- constants: `0`, `0`, `1` ≈ 3
- invocations: `new Map`, `forEach`, `push`, `quotePremium`, `set`, `push`, `insuranceSum`, `processClaim` ≈ 8×2 = 16
- conditionals: `if(quote)`, `quoteCount>0?` = 2×4 = 8
- loops: `forEach` = 5
- assignments: `quoteCount += 1`, `quoteCount=0` (init binding), `let quoteCount` ≈ `+= 1` is 6
- Rough mass ≈ 6+3+16+8+5+6 = 44 — highest mass.

`runScenario` is the orchestrator: highest APP mass (~44) and tied-highest McCabe (4). It's the strongest target. Let me look at it for a measurable improvement.

The function mixes two concerns inline: handling a quote step and handling a claim step. The quote branch does premium calc, policy registration, and counter mutation inline. Extracting the quote-step handling into its own named function would reduce `runScenario`'s McCabe and mass, and improve intent. Let me check the current structure once more and verify whether extraction reduces metrics rather than just moving them.

The `if/else` in `runScenario` is genuine dispatch. The cleanest reduction: extract the quote branch body into a `processQuote` helper mirroring the existing `processClaim`. This removes the `isFollowup` ternary and the `quoteCount += 1` assignment from `runScenario`, but the followup/quoteCount state still needs to live somewhere.

Let me think about whether this actually reduces the worst-offender McCabe. `runScenario` McCabe = 1 (base) + 1 (forEach) + 1 (if) + 1 (`?:`). Extracting the quote body into `processQuote` moves the `?:` out, dropping `runScenario` to McCabe 3 and `processQuote` becomes McCabe 2. That's a net structural win (the max for the pair drops the ternary into a smaller function), and it mirrors `processClaim` for symmetry/intent.

Let me apply this single change.

---ESLINT---
max-params 227 Arrow function has too many parameters (5). Maximum allowed is 4.

content.text: As predicted, this introduced a `max-params` smell (0 → 1). Tests stay green, but the ESLint smell count got **worse**. Per the rules, I revert and try a different angle.

content.content:
Tests  36 passed (36)
   Start at  07:26:43

---ESLINT---
SMELLS: 0

content.text:
Reverted cleanly: 36/36 green, 0 smells. 

**Iteration 1 — Reverted.** Extracting `processQuote` raised ESLint smells 0 → 1 (max-params, 5 params) because the quote branch needs `step`, `customer`, `isFollowup`, `policiesByStep`, and `index` — five inputs. The `if/else` dispatch in `runScenario` is irreducible without violating max-params or hiding intent. Trying a different angle.

## Iteration 2 — Re-examine for a non-regressing improvement

The PRE state is already at the ESLint floor (0 smells), with all cognitive complexity well under threshold and max McCabe = 4. The remaining levers are APP mass reduction and naming, but only where they don't trade off another metric.

Let me reconsider the second-tier candidates. `assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts` (McCabe 4) and `surchargeForItem` (McCabe 4) are both already small, focused, and intent-revealing. `runScenario`'s McCabe-4 is genuine essential complexity (loop + dispatch + first-vs-followup distinction) — not accidental.

Let me look for **cross-file duplication** (Rule 3 — the specific value of this whole-src pass) and a **naming** review against the complete test suite.

**Cross-file duplication check (cli.ts vs claim-office.ts):** cli.ts only does stdin/JSON/stdout/error plumbing; claim-office.ts is pure domain. No shared logic duplicated across files. No extraction opportunity.

**Naming review against the full suite:**

- `quotePremium`, `claimResult`, `processClaim`, `runScenario` — all accurate given the complete behavior.
- `surchargeForItem`, `itemSurcharges`, `policyBasePremium`, `policyModifierRate` — precise.
- `assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts`, `assertNonNegativeAmounts` — precise.
- `damagePayout` — returns `reimbursableAmount - DEDUCTIBLE` for a single damage; accurate.
- `typeBasePremium` — accurate.
- `qualifiesForHalfReimbursement` / `reimbursableAmount` — accurate.

Let me check one APP-mass micro-opportunity that doesn't regress any metric. Look at `surchargeForItem`: it reads `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` and computes two conditional surcharges. The two ternaries (`curse`, `highEnchantment`) are both `rate * base` guarded by a condition. There's a conceptual duplication pattern: `condition ? RATE * base : 0`. Extracting it would add an invocation per call without removing a branch — net APP increase (the reverted-style trade). Not worth it.

Let me verify there are no magic-number or duplicate-string issues hiding and confirm the cognitive complexity scores explicitly by lowering the threshold temporarily to read the actual numbers.

content.text:
This gives me the precise cognitive-complexity scores. The maximum across the whole src/ is **4** (`assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts`, line 175). Everything else is ≤3. The SonarJS threshold is 10, so the entire codebase sits less than half-way to the limit.

Confirmed measured state of the whole src/:

- **ESLint smells: 0**
- **Max cognitive complexity: 4** (`assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts`), all others ≤3 — far below the threshold of 10
- **Max McCabe: 4** (`assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts`, `surchargeForItem`, `runScenario`)
- **Max APP mass: ~44** (`runScenario`), which is essential orchestration cost (loop + dispatch + state)

The single concrete change available (extracting the quote branch from `runScenario`) was tried in Iteration 1 and **regressed** the ESLint smell count via max-params, so it was reverted. Every remaining function is small, single-purpose, intent-revealing, and below every threshold. The only other candidate reductions (extracting the shared `condition ? rate*base : 0` pattern in `surchargeForItem`, or a quote handler) trade a branch removal for added invocation mass with no net McCabe/cognitive benefit and, in the quote case, a new smell.

## Iteration 2 — No Further Improvement Possible

- ESLint smells (whole src/): **0**
- Max cognitive complexity across all functions: **4** (`assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts`); threshold is 10
- Max APP mass: **~44** (`runScenario`) — essential orchestration (forEach + dispatch + first/followup state)
- Max McCabe cyclomatic: **4** (three functions, all essential branching)
- All function names accurately describe their purpose given the full 36-test suite; no cross-file duplication between `cli.ts` and `claim-office.ts`

Any further change either reduces clarity (Rule 2) or trades one metric for another with no net benefit — confirmed empirically by the reverted Iteration 1.

Let me confirm the final state is clean.

content.text:
End-refactor pass complete. The codebase was already at its measured floor after the per-cycle refactors; the one structural change I attempted regressed a metric and was reverted, leaving the source byte-identical to the PRE baseline.

## 🏁 End-Refactor Complete

**Iterations applied: 0 net** (1 attempted, reverted; 1 confirmed-stable)

**Per-iteration summary:**

1. **`runScenario`** — Attempted extracting the quote branch into a `processQuote` helper to drop `runScenario`'s ternary (McCabe 4→3). **Reverted**: the quote branch genuinely needs 5 inputs (`step`, `customer`, `isFollowup`, `policiesByStep`, `index`), so the helper triggered `max-params` → ESLint smells **0 → 1**. Tests stayed green, but the smell regression failed the no-regression rule. Reverted to byte-identical original; re-verified 36/36 green, 0 smells.

2. **Whole-src** — No further improvement possible. Measured the actual cognitive-complexity scores by forcing the threshold to 0: max is **4** (one function), all others ≤3, against a configured limit of 10. Cross-file duplication check (`cli.ts` plumbing vs `claim-office.ts` domain) found none. Naming review against the full 36-test suite found every function name accurate.

**Whole-src final state (measured):**
- Total ESLint smells: **0**
- Max cognitive complexity: **4** — `assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts` (threshold 10)
- Max APP mass: **~44** — `runScenario` (essential: forEach + quote/claim dispatch + first/followup state)
- Max McCabe cyclomatic: **4** — `assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts`, `surchargeForItem`, `runScenario` (all essential branching)

**Tests: 36/36 passing ✅**

The per-cycle refactor agents left the module in already-clean shape: many small single-purpose helpers (`typeBasePremium`, `surchargeForItem`, `policyModifierRate`, `assertNonNegativeAmounts`, `assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts`, `claimResult`, `processClaim`), zero smells, and every metric at or near its floor. The only available structural change traded a smell for a marginal McCabe gain, so the measured-optimal action was to keep the existing design.

Relevant files:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_06-28-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_06-28-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/cli.ts`
